q: The planet Earth is the home to all living and non-living things that are ___ and interdependent on each other to thrive.
The environment contributes greatly to the growth of ___ and other organisms.
Challenges of environmental issues, such as ___ and waste materials from industrial and nuclear energy sectors, must be well informed to the society.
The environment is the surroundings comprising of hydrosphere, lithosphere, ___ and biosphere.
Both natural and man-made chemical substances in the environment have a direct impact on human ___ and development.
In the natural matter cycling, carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and ___ take a variety of chemical forms.
The pathways that allow different chemical substances to move through living and non-living forms and their locations in the biosphere are called a ___ cycle.
The cycling of elements is interconnected with the ___ cycle.
Mineral nutrients are cycled, either rapidly or slowly, through the entire ___ and from one living organism to another.
Most of the Earth’s carbon is stored in ___ and sediments.
The carbon cycle is the biogeochemical cycle by which carbon is exchanged among the biosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and ___ of the Earth.
Plants release ___ when they decay.
___ is the process of gas exchange between the atmosphere and living organisms.
In the fossilisation process, the animal and plant residues become sediment and eventually turn into ___ and minerals.
The combustion of fossil fuels and organic materials gives off carbon dioxide, ___, and energy.
The nitrogen cycle involves the following steps: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, nitrogen ___, ammonification, and denitrification.
Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plants and animals in the form of ions $PO_4^{3-}$ and $HPO_4^{2-}$.
Phosphate salts that are released from rocks through ___ usually dissolve in water present in soil and will be absorbed by plants.
Marine birds play a unique role in the phosphorus cycle, as they take phosphorus-containing fish out of the ocean and return to land, where they ___.
___ is one of the components that make up proteins and vitamins.
When sulphur dioxide enters the atmosphere, it will react with oxygen to produce sulphur trioxide gas ($SO_3$).
Sulphuric acid may also be produced from ___ emitted to the atmosphere by plankton species.
The ocean absorbs much of the carbon dioxide that is released from burning ___.
Extra carbon dioxide is lowering the ocean’s pH, through a process called ___.
The 7 R's of a green environment for sustainable development are Rethink, Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Regift, and ___.
Environmental pollution is caused by the harmful ___ of substances into the environment.
Heavy metals are metals with high ___ and toxic effects even at very low concentrations.
Arsenic is rarely found in nature and is not very toxic, but it can be oxidised easily in humid air and becomes toxic by conversion to ___.
The desorption and dissolution of arsenic from arsenic rich ___ and minerals are the main sources of groundwater contamination and soil.
Sodium arsenate is used to kill some ___ in fruit cultivation and potato growing.
Cadmium is released into the atmosphere predominantly as elemental cadmium and cadmium oxide from some sources as ___.
___ is also a significant source of cadmium exposure.
Cadmium in the environment is also toxic to plants, animals and ___.
___ enters the atmosphere largely in the exhaust fumes from internal combustion engines.
The primary use of lead is in the manufacture of ___.
Elemental mercury occurs in ___.
Inorganic mercury can be transformed into ___ by microorganisms such as phytoplanktons and fungi.
The source of exposure to low levels is mercury released from ___ from the panning of gold and fillings in teeth.
Mercury is generally taken into the body in the form of ___.
A ___ is a substance that kills a pest, or it prevents or reduces the damage a pest may cause.
Pesticides are often grouped into "___" because they act on the pest in the same way.
___ occurs because of the attraction between a chemical and soil particles.
___ is the process of pesticide breakdown after application.
___ is a process by which substances are broken down by the action of sunlight.
POPs are also carbon-based organic compounds that have the special abilities to be ___.
POPs are chemically stable, remain intact in the environment for long periods, and are ___ in nature.
VOCs are organic chemicals that pollute the air and be absorbed or inhaled by humans, animals and ___.
VOCs play a significant role in the formation of ___ and fine particulates in the atmosphere.
The accumulation of ozone, fine particulates and other gaseous pollutants results in ___ that reduces visibility.
The burning of plastics releases toxic gases like ___, furans, mercury and polychlorinated biphenyls into the atmosphere.
Radioactive pollution is the result of released ___ in the environment.
A radionuclide is an atom with in ___ which has excessive energy.
Radioactivity is a spontaneous process of ___ or disintegration of heavy nuclei.
Radiations including X-rays are called ___ radiation.
Alpha particles can be blocked by a few pieces of ___.
Gamma rays are the most difficult to stop and require ___, lead, or other heavy shielding to block them.
The majority of ___ radiation occurs naturally from minerals and a small fraction comes from man-made elements.
___ radiation from space also contributes to the background radiation around us.
Traces of radioactive materials can be found in the food, and water we ___.
Prolonged exposure to ___ gas can cause lung cancer.
Uranium-235, americium-241, and plutonium-239 are ___ materials produced from a breeder reactor.
Ionisation smoke detectors use ___ as a source of alpha particles.
Radiotracers are widely used in medicine, agriculture, ___, industry and civil engineering.
A well-known use of carbon-14 is ___ which can be used to date wooden and organic objects.
Uranium-235 is used as fuel for nuclear power plants and ___ propulsion systems.
Caesium-137 is used as a tracer in medical radiation therapy devices for treating ___.
Nuclear reactors use ___ instead of fossil fuels.
Nuclear bombs are weapons having massive ___ power.
The explosion of a nuclear weapon releases a combination of ___, blast waves and radiation.
Radionuclides found in water include potassium-40, radium-226 and ___.
Water bodies are polluted by accidental leakage of waste material from ___ and thorium mines.
Radioactive materials can enter the human body through water and food and may be accumulated in ___ and certain vital organs.
They cause ___ and cancer.
Background radiation is a measure of the level of ionising radiation naturally present in the environment at a ___.
___ heavy atoms are materials capable of sustaining a nuclear fission chain reaction.
Chemistry plays an essential role to achieve the ___ Sustainable Development Goals.

___ farming that uses fertilisers of organic origin such as compost manure, green manure, and bone meal instead of chemical fertilisers may be one way or another to minimise the environmental problems.
Instead of using chemical-based pesticides, plant-based pesticides that have minimum risks including ___ and insecticidal soaps can be used.
The ___ consist of insecticidal ingredients and they can be used as an insecticide.
To provide nutrients in a plant-available form, agrochemists have developed ___ technology to produce plant-based pesticides.
Plant-based pesticides significantly reduce ___ application rates.
Plant-based insecticidal soaps can be used to eliminate small ___ insects.
The municipal and non-hazardous industrial wastes have been converted to energy by ___ burning.
The process of converting wastes to energy by direct burning is known as ___ or pyrolysis.
In the process of pyrolysis, the organic content wastes in the reactor produce a mixture of hydrogen and ___.
Solar, wind, hydroelectric, biomass, biorefineries, geothermal, and ___ are important resources for future sustainable development.
Ethanol is the most important alcohol which can be produced by converting the sugar content of any ___ material into alcohol.
Productions of ethanol are from ___ materials such as corncob, cornstalk, cornhusk, sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bark.
Biodiesel oil is produced from cultivated ___ oil.
Biodiesel oil is synthesised from plant oils by removing the by-product ___ molecule.
The combustion of fossil fuel generates ___ compounds whereas biodiesel does not.
The main purpose of wastewater treatment is to remove the various ___ that are present in the wastewater.
Photocatalysts binary metal oxides nanomaterials (e.g., $TiO_2, ZnO, Fe_2O_3$) are used to mineralise, decompose, and ___ the pollutants from wastewater.
Previously, ___ (TEL) was used as an anti-knocking agent.
Today, TEL is replaced by using ___ (MTBE).
___ are derived from renewable biomass sources such as corn starch, vegetable oils and fats.
Solvents like ___ ($Cl_2C=CCl_2$) used in dry cleaning of clothes, pollute the groundwater and are carcinogenic.
In the liquid $CO_2$ cleaning process, liquid carbon dioxide and other ___ cleaning materials replace tetrachloroethene.
Large amounts of ___ acid, $HOOC(CH_2)_4COOH$, are used each year for the production of nylon.
___ plating is the method of chromium plating most commonly known as chrome plating.
